chunk_summary: This section discusses therapeutic food availability challenges in India's outpatient CMAM programs, emphasizing protocol reliability, structured adaptations during stock-outs, and standardized visit workflows for managing SAM cases.
learning_objectives: Understand the variability in therapeutic food availability across Indian states and its impact on SAM management.
Learn the importance of predefined adaptation rules during therapeutic food stock-outs.
Familiarize with the standardized outpatient CMAM visit workflow for SAM management.
Recognize the role of WHO 2023 guidelines in dose-reduction protocols for SAM recovery.
key_facts: India lacks a universally adopted therapeutic food product for SAM; states use RUTF, locally produced equivalents, or state-designed supplements.
Inpatient care often relies on locally prepared F-75 and F-100 recipes as per MoHFW guidance.
WHO 2023 framework allows structured dose-reduction only after a child transitions out of SAM criteria and has no nutritional oedema.
Outpatient CMAM visits should include screening for danger signs, appetite verification, weight measurement, and household risk assessment.
Correct triage, cautious medical management, and strict follow-up are critical for SAM treatment success.
reference_quote: Community-based management depends on access to a therapeutic food, yet India has not had a single universally adopted product for SAM across states. (Source: https://www.niti.gov.in/sites/default/files/2023-12/CMAM-report-NITI-Aayog.pdf)
